frameworks.name: theory of mind
frameworks.one_line: Mentalizing about another agent's goal to evaluate observation utility.

frameworks.name: rational utility-based decision-making
frameworks.one_line: Weighs expected utility of social learning against self-exploration.

frameworks.name: social learning
frameworks.one_line: Extended with an explicit decision-to-observe stage rather than reflexive copying.

core_question: How do people decide when to engage in social learning versus non-social self-exploration?
key_assumptions: Social learning carries time and cognitive resource costs
Observers can infer another agent's goal and predict the informativity of their future actions
Learners select actions by comparing expected utilities of social and non-social options
manipulations: Availability and informativity of another player's actions in a multi-player treasure hunt game
Trade-off conditions pitting social observation against self-exploration
key_findings: The rational mentalizing model quantitatively captures human trade-offs between social and non-social learning
Humans flexibly choose whether to observe others based on inferred goal and future-action informativity
interpretations: Combining utility evaluation with theory-of-mind reasoning lets agents apply social learning adaptively to reach goals more efficiently
